summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – APPLICATION FOR A STAY OF INTERIM PARENTING ORDERS – Where appeal has been filed – Where it would be adverse to the children’s interests to order a stay – Where the father’s appeal would not be rendered nugatory by dismissal of his stay application – Where a stay of the appealed orders would cause disturbance to the children’s residence - Refusal to grant a stay pending determination of the appeal – Where the appeal and a final hearing have been listed within eight months of the making of the Orders the subject of appeal – Application dismissed.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ANDERSON J:

INTRODUCTION

These reasons explain the dismissal of the father’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026 seeking an order for the stay of interim parenting orders made by me on 18 December 2025 pending determination of his appeal.

BACKGROUND

The parties to these proceedings are the parents of two (2) male children born in 2025 (“the children”). The children were born as a consequence of a commercial surrogacy arrangement.
The father commenced proceedings under Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) in respect of the children on 21 August 2025.
On 8 September 2025, I heard an ex parte application filed by father seeking orders for the recovery of the children. I made orders with respect to that application on 9 September 2025, and my Reasons for Judgment are published in Warne & Phipps [2025] FedCFamC1F 629.
Subsequently, and by reason of the mother’s Response to an Initiating Application filed on 15 October 2025, I was called upon to determine whether it was appropriate to discharge the ex parte orders made by me. I did so having determined on 18 December 2025 that it is in the best interests of the children to live in the primary care of the mother and spend time with the father on three (3) occasions each week. My reasons for arriving at that decision are published in Warne & Phipps (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 927.
On 18 December 2025, I also made Orders:
(a) Listing the competing applications for a final hearing to commence on 17 August 2026; and

(b) Regulating the parties’ preparation for the final hearing.

The father filed a Notice of Appeal in respect of my decision on 8 January 2026. Despite this, the father did not file an application seeking an order for the stay of the parenting orders made by me until 15 January 2026.
The father’s appeal in respect of my orders is listed before the Full Court of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division One) on 29 April 2026.
These Reasons explain why I have decided to dismiss the father’s Stay Application pending determination of the appeal.

EVIDENCE

The father relied on the following:
(c) An Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026;

(d) An Affidavit in the name of the father filed on 15 January 2026;

(e) An Affidavit in the name of Ms R filed on 11 February 2026;

(f) A Notice of Appeal filed on 8 January 2026;

(g) Some tendered documents;[1] and

(h) An Outline of Case Document filed on 6 March 2026.

The mother relied on the following:
(a) A Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 19 February 2026;

(b) An Affidavit in the name of the mother filed on 19 February 2026;

(c) Some tendered documents;[2] and

(d) An Outline of Case Document filed on 6 March 2026.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer relied on an Outline of Case Document filed on 16 March 2026.
The Court excluded all annexures to the affidavits having regard to r 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”).

APPLICABLE LAW

Rule 13.12 of the Rules provides parties with the opportunity to apply for a stay of an order of the Court pending the outcome of an appeal.
As the judge who made the order under appeal, I have jurisdiction to hear and determine the application for a stay of the orders. The principles relating to applications for a stay of orders are those described by Austin J in Zyma & Begum [2025] FedCFamC1A 11 at [15] to [16], as follows:[3]
The discretion to stay the operation of appealed orders should only be exercised where circumstances exist to justify departure from the ordinary rule that a successful litigant is entitled to the fruits of the litigation pending the determination of any appeal. Such circumstances justifying a stay will exist where it is necessary to prevent the appeal from being rendered nugatory or for whatever other reason there is a real risk it will not be possible for a successful appellant to be restored substantially to his or her former position if the judgment against him or her is executed (Federal Commissioner of Taxation v Myer Emporium Ltd (No 1) [1986] HCA 13; (1986) 160 CLR 220 at 222–223). The Court should also consider the prospects of the appeal and where the balance of convenience lies (see Jennings Constructions Ltd v Burgundy Royale Investments Pty Ltd (No 1) [1986] HCA 84; (1986) 161 CLR 681 at 685).
Those common law principles apply equally to judgments delivered in this jurisdiction, including judgments in parenting proceedings (JRN & IEG [1998] HCATrans 263; (1998) 72 ALJR 1329; Sheldon & Weir (Stay Application) [2011] FamCAFC 5 at [14]–[15]; Aldridge v Keaton (Stay Appeal) [2009] FamCAFC 106 at [18]; Trahn & Long (No 2) [2008] FamCAFC 194 at [38]). In relation to the proposed stay of appealed parenting orders, the welfare of the child is now considered a significant, but not the paramount, consideration. Residential changes should desirably be limited as far as reasonably possible. The Court should also consider whether the child’s present circumstances are satisfactory, the bona fides of the appeal, the apparent strength of the appeal, and the likely delay before the appeal is heard and determined.
The Full Court in Aldridge & Keaton (Stay Appeal) [2009] FamCAFC 106 (“Aldridge & Keaton (Stay Appeal)”) confirmed the discretionary nature of the determination of an application for a stay pending appeal and identified eleven (11) principles applicable to a stay application.[4] Those principles were adopted and applied in another Full Court decision in Cantrell & North & Anor (2019) FLC 93-921; [2019] FamCAFC 127 (“Cantrell’).[5]
More recently, the principles applicable to an application for a stay were described by Austin J in Navickas & Fried [2024] FedCFamC1A 248 (“Navickas”) as follows:
The power of the appeal court to stay the orders the subject of the appeal is found in s 38(1)(b) of the FCFCA Act.
The discretion to stay the operation of appealed orders should only be exercised where circumstances exist to justify departure from the ordinary rule that a successful litigant is entitled to the fruits of the litigation pending the determination of any appeal. Such circumstances justifying a stay will exist where it is necessary to prevent the appeal from being rendered nugatory or for whatever other reason there is a real risk it will not be possible for a successful appellant to be restored substantially to his or her former position if the judgment against him or her is executed (Federal Commissioner of Taxation v Myer Emporium Ltd (No 1) [1986] HCA 13; (1986) 160 CLR 220 at 222–223). The Court should also consider the prospects of the appeal and where the balance of convenience lies (see Jennings Constructions Ltd v Burgundy Royale Investments Pty Ltd (No 1) [1986] HCA 84; (1986) 161 CLR 681 at 685).
Those common law principles apply equally to judgments delivered in this jurisdiction, including judgments in parenting proceedings (JRN & IEG [1998] HCATrans 263; (1998) 72 ALJR 1329; Sheldon & Weir (Stay Application) [2011] FamCAFC 5 at [14]–[15]; Aldridge v Keaton (Stay Appeal) [2009] FamCAFC 106 at [18]; Trahn & Long (No 2) [2008] FamCAFC 194 at [38]). In relation to the proposed stay of appealed parenting orders, the welfare of the children is now considered a significant, but not the paramount, consideration. Residential changes should desirably be limited as far as reasonably possible. The Court should also consider whether the children’s present circumstances are satisfactory, the bona fides of the appeal, the apparent strength of the appeal, and the likely delay before the appeal is heard and determined.
The Navickas exposition of principles applicable to a stay application demonstrates that it is not necessary to separately address every one of the eleven principles set out in Aldridge & Keaton (supra).

DISPOSITION

The mother is entitled to presume that my discretionary judgment is correct.[6] As identified by Justice Austin in Bradshaw & Bradshaw (No 3) [2023] FedCFamC1F 510:
The discretion to stay the operation of orders should only be exercised where circumstances exist which justify departure from the ordinary rule that a successful litigant is entitled to the fruits of his or her litigation pending the determination of any appeal (Federal Commissioner of Taxation v Myer Emporium (No. 1) [1986] HCA 13; (1986) 160 CLR 220 at 222).
At this juncture, I am not satisfied that the evidence demonstrates a lack of bona fides in the father’s application. The mother invites me to draw such an inference in circumstances where:
(a) The Application for a Stay was not filed until 15 January 2026 being twenty-eight (28) days after the delivery of my Reasons for Judgment and Orders on 18 December 2025; and

(b) The Application for a Stay and Affidavit in support were not served on the mother’s solicitor until 23 January 2026.

Notwithstanding the force in the submissions made by the mother’s counsel, I am not satisfied that the evidence demonstrates a lack of bona fides or mala fides on the part of the father. The chronology surrounding the father’s making of the Stay Application may give rise to possible inferences being drawn, which I am invited by the mother’s counsel to make but I am not prepared to make such a finding on an interlocutory basis.
In relation to the strength of his appeal, and by his Notice of Appeal document, the father identifies twenty (20) grounds of appeal. I replicate the grounds of appeal below:
That his Honour erred in making a material findings of fact at interim hearing that the Respondent was the primary carer of the children. As a result of this conclusion on a highly controversial factual issue in the interim proceedings, his Honour made parenting orders contrary to the best interests of the children.
That his Honour erred at law in failing to consider the impact of removing the children from the Appellant’s care and their attendance at early education, which is contrary to the legislative pathway governing the best interests of the children.
That his Honour erred at law in failing to consider the possible final outcomes of this parenting proceeding at final hearing, noting that this is an international commercial surrogacy parenting matter. As a result, his Honour made parenting orders contrary to the best interests of the children.
That his Honour did not adequately consider the legislative pathway, the principles applicable to children, and the law governing international commercial surrogacy of children in his interim decision, which resulted in parenting orders made contrary to the best interests of the children.
That his Honour erred in making material findings of fact at interim hearing about the nature of the relationship between the Appellant and Respondent, which in turn, resulted in his Honour concluding that the Appellant was dishonest with the Court.
That his Honour erred in making material findings of fact at interim hearing at paragraph 52(c) of his Judgment, that the Appellant did not tell the Court at the ex parte hearing of 8 September 2025 he left the children with the Respondent on 15 August 2025. In turn, his Honour incorrectly concluded that the Appellant was dishonest with the Court at the ex parte hearing on 8 September 2025.
That his Honour erred in making material findings of controversial facts at interim hearing about the ‘Mother’s Day Card’ referred to at paragraph 52(d) of his Judgment, and draws inferences against the Appellant. In turn, his Honour incorrectly concluded that the Appellant was dishonest with the Court at the ex parte hearing on 8 September 2025.
That his Honour erred in making a material finding of controversial facts at interim hearing at paragraph 68(e) of his Judgment, that the Respondent was unable to settle the children at the police station [in August 2025] because there was no food, in contrast to his Honour’s finding on the evidence of the police body worn camera footage admitted into evidence at the ex parte hearing that the Respondent was unable to settle the children because she had limited insight and no capacity to soothe the children. As a result, his Honour incorrectly conclude that the Respondent had capacity to meet the needs of the children, and made parenting orders for the children to live with the Respondent.
That his Honour erred in making a material finding of fact at interim hearing, at paragraph 74 of his Judgment, that the Appellant had no capacity to care for the children contrary to his evidence at the ex parte hearing, because the children were placed into day care after the children commenced living with the Appellant on 12 September 2025. As a result, his Honour incorrectly determined that the Appellant lacked capacity to care for the children and deemed him dishonest.
That his Honour made an error of law at interim hearing in placing too much weight on the Respondent’s report from [Dr S] (Psychiatrist) to satisfy himself that the Respondent did not present with a psychiatric condition. As a result, his Honour dismissed the Appellant’s concerns about the Respondent’s mental health conditions and the risks the children were exposed to in the Respondent’s care.
That his Honour erred in making a material finding of fact at interim hearing that the Appellant knew about the letter from the Respondent’s solicitors dated 5 September 2025, and did not bring said letter to the attention of his Honour at the time of the ex parte hearing on 8 September 2025. Consequently, his Honour made findings inconsistent with the evidence and concluded that the Appellant was dishonest with the Court at the ex parte hearing on 8 September 2025.
That his Honour erred in making a material finding of fact at interim hearing, at paragraphs 19, 70(a) and 71(a), that at no stage prior to 9 September 2025, 5 November 2025 or 25 November 2025, did the Appellant or his solicitors write to his Honour’s Chambers to relist the proceedings after becoming aware the Respondent was legally represented. As a result of his Honour making these findings unavailable on the evidence, his Honour concluded that the Appellant was dishonest in his conduct and evidence.
That his Honour erred in making a material finding of fact at interim hearing, at paragraphs 21-22, that the Appellant had knowledge the Respondent was an Australian Citizen, had no ties to India, that she always lived and worked in Australia, and has no family in India. As a result of his Honour making this finding which was not available on the evidence, his Honour fell into error in concluding that the Appellant was dishonest with the Court and the Respondent did not pose a flight risk to the children.
That his Honour did not adequately assess the objective evidence admitted to examine the unacceptable risk of harm the Respondent posed to the children.
That his Honour did not consider the Affidavit evidence of paternal uncle, [Mr T], and failed to provide any reasons why such evidence was not considered in his Judgment following interim hearing. In turn, his Honour erred in failing to properly assess risk of harm to the children as contended by the Appellant at interim hearing.
That his Honour did not consider the Appellant’s evidence at interim hearing in relation to the Respondent leaving sharps around and exposing the children to a hazardous environment. As a result of failing to consider this evidence, his Honour did not engage in an adequate assessment of risk in the best interests of the children.
That his Honour did not adequately take into account the absence of evidence from the Respondent at interim hearing about how she was caring for the children at [an address at [Suburb P] in the State of New South Wales] between 15 August 2025 to 12 September 2025. As a result, his Honour did not adequately scrutinise the Respondent’s case to assess the risks of harm to the children and place weight on the Appellant’s concerns, consequently resulting in parenting orders contrary to the best interests of the children.
That his Honour did not carefully scrutinise the weight to be attached to the letter from [Dr U] to determine whether it was a draft, the contents was correct, or it was a final version. His Honour therefore incorrectly concluded that the contents of the letter was correct, and preferred this evidence above the photographs tendered by the Appellant dated 12 September 2025. As a result, his Honour did not adequately assess the risk of harm to the children while they were in the Respondent’s care and made parenting orders contrary to their best interests.
That his Honour did not address the Respondent’s failure to provide to provide full and frank disclosure prior to the interim hearing, and draw any adverse inferences against the Respondent at the interim hearing as he had done so against the Appellant. Therefore, his Honour did not adequately balance the evidence about each party’s disclosure and information provided to the Court, and made parenting orders in the Respondent’s favour.
That his Honour did not give adequate reasons in his interim decision from departing from his factual findings of the objective evidence admitted at the ex parte hearing on 8 September 2025 concluding that the Respondent posed an unacceptable risk of harm to the children.
Grounds 10, 14, 17, 18 and 19 of the appeal involve an assessment of weight only and/or an assertion that I failed to adequately balance the competing contentions of the parties at an abridged hearing.
In House v The King (1936) 55 CLR 409 at 504-505, the majority of the High Court said:
The manner in which an appeal against an exercise of discretion should be determined is governed by established principles. It is not enough that the judges composing the appellate court consider that, if they had been in the position of the primary judge, they would have taken a different course. It must appear that some error has been made in exercising the discretion. If the judge acts upon a wrong principle, if he allows extraneous or irrelevant matters to guide or affect him, if he mistakes the facts, if he does not take into account some material consideration, then his determination should be reviewed and the appellate court may exercise its own discretion in substitution for his if it has the materials for doing so.

(emphasis added)
A reading of Grounds 10, 14, 17, 18 and 19 do not assert that I applied a wrong principle, taken into account extraneous or irrelevant matters, made a mistake of fact or failed to take into account some material consideration. Instead, the mother’s counsel conceded that the grounds involved an assessment of matters of weight only and that this being so, the father faced the difficulties referred to by the High Court in Gronow & Gronow (1979) 144 CLR 513. As was stated in that decision at [10]:
...an appellate court should be slow to overturn a primary judge’s discretionary decision on grounds which only involve conflicting assessments of matters of weight.
Accordingly, and whilst I accept that the father has “an arguable case”, I consider that in the absence of those factors referred to above, an appellate court may be slow to overturn my discretionary decision on grounds which involve conflicting assessments of matters of weight only.
I do not understand the gravamen of Grounds 3 or 4 of the Notice of Appeal. The decisions published by me on 9 September 2025 and 18 December 2025 specifically identify that the children were born as a consequence of a commercial surrogacy agreement entered into in Country D. The father’s counsel did not advise me of an authority, which required me to consider the competing parenting applications by an approach other than the approach, which I am mandated to follow by statute, namely, section 60CC of the Act. I do not consider that the father has an arguable case with respect to Grounds 3 or 4 of the appeal.
Neither the mother’s counsel nor counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer conceded that the father has by reference to the remaining Grounds of Appeal established arguable grounds for relief. Particularly, it is not conceded by the mother that I made any findings, which were inconsistent with the evidence before me.
For these reasons, I consider that there is a possibility that the father’s appeal will fail. Even if this conclusion is erroneous, I find that the father will not be personally prejudiced by the refusal of the stay application. This is because the appeal is to be heard by the Full Court within five (5) weeks and during the intervening period, my Orders provide for the children to spend time with the father on three (3) occasions each week in any event.
The father’s counsel asserts that a refusal of the stay application will be prejudicial to the children because it is not in their interests to remain in the primary care of the mother. Particularly, the father contends that:
(a) The mother “posed, and continues to pose, a risk to the children”;[7]

(b) The orders made by me on 18 December 2025 have caused “the children to be exposed to further neglect, psychological harm and conflict between the parties”;[8] and

(c) It is “apparent prima facie that the children continue to face...exposure to risk under the mother’s care indicative that their best interests are not being considered”.[9]

In support of his argument, the father says that:
(a) On 27 December 2025, he “observed that [the youngest child] had a substantial amount of crusty dirt in his ears, which is as a result of his ears not being cleaned”.[10] The father says that he took photographs of the child’s ears and cleaned them. He annexed to his affidavit three (3) photographs to support his assertion;

(b) On 1 January 2026, the children were not wearing clothing appropriate for a temperature of nineteen (19) degrees. The father says that when changing the children into warmer clothing, he noticed that the youngest child’s “pants were soaking wet with pee and poo” and that “[t]he nappy was overflowing”. The father took photographs in circumstances where he says that this “was yet another example of severe neglect from [the mother] and not attending to the children’s basic needs”;[11]

(c) On 1 January 2026, he observed the children “crawling, walking, talking, and playing with their toys”. He says that on his observation, “both children had regressed”;[12]

(d) On 3 January 2026, the mother dressed the children in “warm clothes...despite it being a warm and humid day of about twenty-six (26) degrees”. He again complains the children’s “ears were filthy and clogged with dirt and earwax” and in addition, he complains that the children had “long sharp fingernails”;[13]

(e) On 6 January 2026, he observed “severe sores on [the eldest child’s] arm”. The father says that contrary to representations made by the mother, the sores “did not look like mosquito bites” and were “filled with pus”;[14] and

(f) The mother has a negative attitude with respect to the father and this being so, exposes the children to psychological harm.[15]

The father’s concern to the effect that the mother is neglecting the children and/or acting in a manner, which is not consistent with their best interests is not supported by objective albeit untested evidence. I summarise that evidence below.
Firstly, the elder child’s treating general practitioner examined him in March 2026 for the purposes of what the general practitioner describes as the child’s “11-month well-child check”. Having examined him, the general practitioner says as follows with respect to the child’s general progress:
He is a happy and alert little boy who is developing wonderfully. All his growth measurements, including his height (77cm), weight (10.56kg), and head circumference (47 cm), are tracking perfectly along the 75th percentile for his age.[16]
The general practitioner also expressed a view that the child was “meeting all the expected milestones for an 11-month-old”. Insofar as the father said that he observed a regression in the eldest child’s motor skills when he spent time with him on 6 January 2026, the eldest child’s treating general practitioner identified that the child was “developing his fine motor skills” and “building his strength by pulling himself up to stand and cruising around his playpen”.[17]
From a physical perspective and having undertaken a “top-to-toe examination”, the eldest child’s treating general practitioner expressed an opinion that “everything was found to be normal”. He also said as follows:
(a) The child’s ears “are a little waxy”. However, the general practitioner said that “[t]his is very common and normal in babies and does not require any treatment”; and

(b) “Overall... [the child] is a healthy and thriving baby who is right on track with his development”.[18]

The youngest child’s “11-month developmental check-up” was similarly positive. Particularly, the child’s treating general practitioner expressed an opinion that the youngest child is “an alert, active and happy baby”. In a manner similar to his brother, the general practitioner said that the child’s “growth is tracking perfectly” and he is “meeting all his milestones”.[19]
With respect to the eldest child, the views of his treating general practitioner are supported by his treating paediatrician who by letter dated 20 February 2026 expressed an opinion that:
(a) The child “had no unusual skin markings, normal cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal exam and normal genitalia, hips and otherwise looked well”; and

(b) The child “seems very on track with growth and development”.[20]

The report identified above was served on the father’s solicitor on 27 February 2026.[21] Despite the positive nature of the report, the father by his counsel’s Case Outline Document continued to assert that the eldest child is being neglected by his mother.
At an interim stage, the weight of the evidence does not support the father’s contentions. Further, and insofar as the father asserts that the sores on the eldest child’s arm described above were caused by staphylococcus bacteria, the father failed to provide any evidence to support his assertion.
A further argument advanced by the father is to the effect that because the children are not attending at day care, the children have been “set...back in achieving their developmental milestones”.[22] In support of that proposition, the father refers to an affidavit in the name of Ms R filed on 11 February 2026. Ms R is the “Service Manager” at an Early Learning Centre previously attended by the children. The terms of the affidavit provide a commendatory opinion of the father’s parenting capacity. By her affidavit, Ms R also expresses an opinion that when the father first enrolled the children at the centre in September 2025, the children “were very well behind in their development”.[23] Even if such an opinion is correct, I consider the opinion to carry limited weight given the more recent reports of the children’s treating general practitioner and the eldest child’s paediatrician (supra).
I also consider that the father’s expressed concern about the children not attending at day care is contrary to his refusal to permit the mother to enrol the children in daycare on two (2) days per week for a period of five (5) hours on each occasion so that she might pursue employment. Particularly, and despite his apparent concern that the children have developmentally regressed since having been withdrawn from their Early Learning facility, the mother says that the father has refused to provide his consent to the children starting daycare.[24]
In Chiles & Petrenko [2024] FedCFamC1A 112 at [32], Justice Tree said as follows:
Orthodox risk assessment and management in parenting proceedings has been the subject of explanation in several full court authorities, most notably in Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092). It bears repeating that the usual course in undertaking the assessment is to identify the contended risk event, gauge the likelihood of it occurring, and then consider the magnitude of harm which might ensue if the risk event does occur. One then considers whether in the light of those matters the risk of harm is acceptable or not.
Simply, and pending final hearing, I am not satisfied that the risk of harm presented by the mother is such that their living arrangements ought to be varied.
Further, and whatever the nature and extent of the father’s concerns, the father by his counsel’s Outline of Case Document made a submission in the following terms:
In the event the Court declines a Stay of the current parenting Orders as sought by the father in this application, it is respectfully submitted a Stay could be granted conditional on a variation to the Interim Orders that would permit the children to spend overnight time in the father’s care from 4.00pm Friday to 4.00pm Saturday in week one and 4.00pm Saturday to 4.00pm Sunday in week two, in addition to the current weekdays (Tuesdays and Thursdays) prescribed by the Interim Orders of 18 December 2025, and further times when the mother is not available to have the children in her care.[25]
Such a proposal is inconsistent with the father’s assertion that the children will be exposed “to further neglect, [and] psychological harm” in the care of the mother.
There is a further matter, which causes me grave concern when considering the best interests of the children and that is the conflict between the parties. That conflict is exemplified by the sending of eight (8) solicitor letters in two (2) business days by the father’s solicitor, such letters sent between 12.28pm on 9 March 2026 and 2.22pm on 10 March 2026. In response, and by letter dated 16 March 2026, the mother’s solicitor said as follows:
At the outset, we express concerns that the ongoing excessive course of correspondence and positions advanced by your client risk operating in a matter that is procedurally oppressive and unnecessarily escalating costs of these proceedings.[26]
I make no finding as to the conduct of either party or his/her solicitor. However, and pending receipt of a Family Report as ordered by me on 18 December 2025, I have limited confidence that the parties have the capacity to co-parent within the terms of the parenting arrangement proposed by the father (supra).
Finally, I recognise that changes in the children’s primary living arrangements should be minimised. A stay of my Orders would be incompatible with that objective by requiring the children to now return to the father’s primary care. If the father’s appeal ultimately fails, there will be another change of the children’s living arrangements. If the stay is refused and the father’s appeal succeeds, only one change of residence back to the father from the mother will be required. For these reasons, I agree with submissions made by the mother’s counsel that there would be “nothing worse for these two young boys around toddler age to have their adults play jurisdictional ping pong”.
When arriving at such a conclusion, I am mindful of the decision of Justice Austin in Bradshaw & Bradshaw (No 3) [2023] FedCFamC1F 510:
Any stay of the appealed orders would result in another disturbance to the child’s residence, which binding authority suggests should be avoided. If the appeal ultimately succeeds then, upon either the re-exercise of discretion or the remitter of the dispute for re-hearing, an order may be made for the child to be returned to the father’s residential care.[27]
For these reasons, I dismiss the father’s application for a stay of the parenting orders made by me on 18 December 2025.
By her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 19 February 2026, the mother sought an order that the father pay her costs of and incidental to his stay application on an indemnity basis. I will reserve the mother’s costs of the application. Similarly, I will reserve the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer. I direct that any application and affidavit in support with respect to the question of costs be filed within twenty-eight (28) days of the determination, withdrawal or abandonment of the appeal in relation to my final orders.
